0.name: Prof. Candice
0.text: Now that the assessment is out of the way, let's proceed to the 3rd Law of Motion!

1.name: Prof. Candice
1.text: It is all about the interaction of two objects exerting force on each other that is equal in magnitude and opposite in direction.

2.name: Prof. Candice
2.text: According to Newton's own statement, one that is translated from Latin of the Principia, is:

3.text: "For every action, there is always an equal but opposite reaction."

4.name: Prof. Candice
4.text: And the general formula for it is FA on FB = →FB on FA

5.name: Prof. Candice
5.text: Let's visualize an apple sitting in equilibrium on a table.

6.name: Prof. Candice
6.text: What forces are on the table? What is the reaction force to each of the forces acting on it?

7.name: Prof. Candice
7.text: And finally, what are the action-reaction pairs?

8.name: Prof. Candice
8.text: In this example, we can see the two forces acting on the apple →F(Earth on the Apple) is the weight of the apple

9.name: Prof. Candice
9.text: Similarly, the →F(Table on the Apple) is the upward normal force exerted by the table on the apple.

10.name: Prof. Candice
10.text: As we can see, the two forces acting on the apple are →F(Earth on Apple) + →F(Table on Apple).

11.name: Prof. Candice
11.text: Because the apple is still in equilibrium; it doesn't move, it's at rest, correct?

12.name: Prof. Candice
12.text: So, the net force must be zero. We can also write it in formal statement as:

15.name: Prof. Candice
15.text: As you can see, we just swapped our variables similar to the other part of the equation but this time, it's negative.

16.name: Prof. Candice
16.text: Also, as the table pushes up on the apple, the corresponding reaction is the downward force exerted on the table by the apple,

17.name: Prof. Candice
17.text: And the action-reaction pair for this is the same as the previous but since our variables are “table” and “apple”, it will be:

19.name: Prof. Candice
19.text: Comparing all of the three, as you can notice, the forces acting on the apple,

20.name: Prof. Candice
20.text: F(Earth on Apple) and  →F(Table on Apple), are not action-reaction pairs.

21.name: Prof. Candice
21.text: Can anyone tell me why you think so, class?

22.text: [font size=16]Why are F(Earth on Apple) and  →F(Table on Apple) not action-reaction pairs?[/font]

23.1.name: Prof. Candice
23.1.text: Exactly, because none of the formal statements gives the opposite reaction.

23.2.name: Prof. Candice
23.2.text: No, it's actually because none of the formal statements gives the opposite reaction.

24.name: Colin
24.text: Is this because the formal statements are the forces, and these forces are both acting on the same object?

25.name: Colin
25.text: Based on my observation, the action-reaction pairs never act on the same objects.

26.name: Colin
26.text: For example, being knocked back from shoving an object, there's a force applied to the object and bounced back to the body.

27.name: Prof. Candice
27.text: Mhm, indeed. That is a very good observation, Mr. Colin. Looks like you really do pay attention to my class after all.

28.name: Prof. Candice
28.text: Now, let's have a little demonstration.

29.name: Prof. Candice
29.text: Colin? Why don't you try shoving this box? I can assure you, it's not going anywhere far.
